config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I do not think society should automatically come before the rights of the individual, because once you give the state or the majority too much power, individual freedoms can be easily abused. In my view, a just society protects basic rights first, then balances them with public needs only when there is a clear and limited reason. From my perspective, collective interests matter, but they should not become an excuse to silence or sacrifice people unfairly.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not believe the interests of society should automatically take priority over the rights of the individual. From a U.S. perspective, that idea makes me cautious because history shows how easily “the public good” can be used to excuse censorship, discrimination, or government overreach. I do think individual rights have limits when they directly harm others, but those limits should be narrow, justified, and subject to strong checks. A healthy society is not one where the majority can override people at will; it is one where basic rights are protected even when that is inconvenient.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not automatically take priority over the rights of the individual. In Indonesia, I have seen how appeals to harmony, order, or the common good can sometimes be used to pressure people into silence, especially when the minority has less power than the majority. Society does matter, and rights are not absolute when they clearly harm others, but the burden should be on society to prove a real and limited need before restricting anyone’s freedom. If we reverse that principle, it becomes too easy for governments or majorities to justify abuse in the name of the public good.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not believe the interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule. From a United States perspective, I am wary of any argument that lets the majority or the state override basic rights in the name of the common good, because that has been used to justify censorship, segregation, and other abuses. Society does matter, but it should only limit individual rights in narrow cases where there is clear harm to others and strong legal safeguards. If we put society first by default, it becomes too easy to excuse unfair treatment of people who are unpopular or powerless.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take automatic priority over the rights of the individual. From my perspective in Indonesia, I have seen how “public order” and “harmony” can become convenient excuses to pressure dissenters, minorities, or anyone who does not fit the majority view. Society absolutely matters, but if we start by assuming the collective is always more important, then individual rights become fragile and easy to sacrifice whenever it is politically convenient. The better rule is the opposite: protect basic rights first, and only limit them when there is a clear, specific, and necessary reason.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not believe the interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual as a default rule. In the United States, I would be very skeptical of any claim that the common good justifies overriding basic rights, because that logic has too often been used to excuse censorship, discrimination, and government overreach. Society absolutely has legitimate interests, but those interests should only limit individual rights in narrow cases where there is clear harm to others and strong legal safeguards. If we start by putting society first, the people who lose out are usually the unpopular, the minority, or the powerless.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
